stages.notes: Often relationship-driven: campus recruiting, seminars, or CV posted online. PhD-level quant roles; rolling hires through the year.
Three separate phone rounds (~45–60 min each). Probability, statistics, calculus, linear algebra, and programming. Communication is scored; asking for hints is normal and expected.
Multi-day data exercise (typically a few days to a week). Python/pandas on trading or market-style datasets. You finish on your own timeline — not a live timed test.
~5 hours: advanced math, computing, logic puzzles, and culture fit. Intern loop mirrors full-time; internship adds a project with presentations toward return offers.

processNotes: Finance background is not required upfront — math and probability strength matters more.
Public prep narratives cite Ross (Introduction to Probability Models), Bailey (Probability & Statistical Models — problem-solving focus), and introductory derivatives pricing texts.
Phone rounds assess how you think out loud; partial progress with good hints beats silent perfection.

questions.text: Walk through a classic conditional-probability puzzle (e.g. Monty Hall or a variant with changing information). Explain your reasoning step by step.
Solve a probability or expectation problem involving dice, cards, or urns. The interviewer may give hints — practice narrating your thought process while you work.
Linear algebra or calculus question at the level of eigenvalues, matrix intuition, or optimization — explain concepts, not just compute.
Short programming exercise or live coding: implement a small algorithm or data structure and talk through complexity and edge cases.
Take-home: load a trading or market-style dataset with Python/pandas, clean anomalies, and compute summary statistics or signals the prompt specifies. Document assumptions.
Onsite logic puzzle or brainteaser — often unrelated to finance. Show structured reasoning and sanity checks.
Deeper math or computing problem under time pressure — may combine probability with implementation sketch.
Culture fit: how you collaborate on hard problems, handle ambiguity, and communicate technical work to non-specialists.
Recruiter screen: walk through your PhD or research background and why you are moving from academia to a trading desk.

sources.title: Public PhD → prop-shop career narrative (firm anonymized here)
